items.q: How are most microplastics formed in the environment?
items.correct: Larger plastic items break down physically into smaller fragments over time.
items.misconception: They are all manufactured at microscopic size deliberately.
items.why: Students think microplastics are designed small. Most are SECONDARY - formed when sun, waves, and wear break down larger items. Primary microplastics (microbeads, fibres) are a smaller proportion.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: Which cycle has no significant atmospheric phase?
items.why: It's Phosphorus. “Carbon” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Average time in a reservoir is called?
items.why: It's Residence time. “Flux” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate residence time of CO2 in the atmosphere in years (centennial estimate)?
items.why: Exact answer: 100 years. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
